title: Merged 12 Fundamental Existential Prerequisites (12FEP) — Längle-Grounded Codebook
purpose: Score participant testimony from psychological interventions on the twelve existential prerequisites that operationalize Längle’s four Existential Fundamental Motivations (EFMs). All constructs, indicators, and quotes are drawn from Längle’s works.

anchors.0: Not present; opposite experience predominates (e.g., “restlessness, insecurity and fear”; “longing… coldness… depression”; “solitude… shame”; “emptiness… despair”).
anchors.1: Barely present; fleeting or fragile signs; coping reactions dominate (e.g., “avoidance or flight… overactivity… destructive aggression… denial”; “regression”).
anchors.2: Partly present; intermittent signs without stable appropriation (“it is not enough to find… I also have to… accept”).
anchors.3: Moderately present; clear signs plus some active stance (e.g., “to accept… and to endure”; “turn to… get into touch”).
anchors.4: Strongly present; consistent experience and appropriation with minor gaps.
anchors.5: Fully present; stable experience and corresponding active stance (e.g., “to be there and not to flee”; “consent to being touched”; “say ‘yes to myself’”; “attitude of openness… ‘What does this hour want from me?’”).

global_instructions_for_llms: Use only concepts and phrasing grounded in Längle’s texts. Every score must be justified with (1) a participant quote and (2) a short direct quote from Längle placed in the item’s “anchor_quotes”/“active_stance_quote.”
Treat the four cornerstones—world, life, person, meaning—as structuring motivation and calling for “inner consent.” Identify both experiential evidence and active stance.
Meaning: apply Längle’s definition of meaning as “the most valuable, realistic possibility of the given situation, for which I feel I should decide myself.”

When evidence is mixed, favor the lower score unless the participant shows the relevant active stance (accept/endure; turn to/consent to being touched; say yes to self/encounter/delimit; openness to what life asks).
Use negative indicators and coping patterns to justify low scores (0–1) and the presence of outcomes (trust/fundamental value/self-esteem/fulfillment) to justify higher scores.

example.participant_excerpt: I protect Friday evenings for my kids—nothing overrides that.
presence_or_absence_indicators_matched: Deliberately taking time for what is valuable
example.langle_anchor_quote: “What do I take time for?… To take time for something means to give away a part of one’s life.”
example.rationale_linking_excerpt_to_quote: The practice shows conscious giving of life-time to what is valuable, matching Längle’s criterion.

validation_checks: Internal consistency within each EFM (Protection/Space/Support; Relationship/Time/Closeness; Attention/Justice/Appreciation; Field/Structure/Value).
Sequential logic: very high E4 with very low E1–E3 should be examined.
Coping alignment: basic/activism/aggression/freezing patterns should align with lower scores on the corresponding EFM.

items.name: Protection
anchor_quotes: “This demands three things: protection, space and support.”
“Do I enjoy protection, acceptance, do I feel at home somewhere?”
“The sum of these experiences of trust is the fundamental trust.”
indicators_present: Acceptance/belonging; “feel at home.”
Speaks of “trust in the world and confidence in myself.”
indicators_absent: Coping: “avoidance or flight… overactivity… destructive aggression… denial.”
items.active_stance_quote: “To accept… to rely on the support and to trust the protection; in short ‘to be there’ and not to flee.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable felt protection plus explicit acceptance/‘being there.’

items.name: Space
anchor_quotes: “Do I have enough space for being there?”
“To accept means to be ready to occupy the space.”
indicators_present: Room to exist/act; readiness to “occupy the space.”
Ability “to let each other be, because there is still enough space for me.”
indicators_absent: Denial or pretending to be non-existent.
items.active_stance_quote: “To accept… to be ready to occupy the space.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Consistently takes and holds space in life contexts.

items.name: Support
anchor_quotes: “Where do I find support in my life?”
“Fundamental trust… the last support in my life.”
indicators_present: Identifies reliable supports; relies on them.
“Trust in the world and confidence in myself… faith in God.”
items.active_stance_quote: “To rely on the support… and… not to flee.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable supports and explicit reliance in action/decisions.

items.name: Relationship
items.fundamental_question: “I am alive—do I like this fact?”
anchor_quotes: “In order to seize my life, to love it, I need three things: relationship, time and closeness.”
“Do I have relationships… in which I experience community?”
indicators_present: Turns toward others: “turn to… get into touch.”
Harmony with world/self; “depth of life.”
indicators_absent: Coping: regression/achievement/overprotection; rage to agitate the other.
items.active_stance_quote: “I seize life… when I turn to other people… get into touch…”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Sustained, reciprocal relationships with active turning-toward.

items.name: Time
items.fundamental_question: “I am alive—do I like this fact?”
anchor_quotes: “What do I take time for?… To take time for something means to give away a part of one’s life.”
indicators_present: Deliberately gives time to “valuable” things; protects time.
Reports harmony and valuing of life.
indicators_absent: “Shallowness of daily life… negligence… make life stale.”
Depressive tone; reactive rage about time.
items.active_stance_quote: “To take time… means to give away a part of one’s life.”

scoring_guidance.0_1: No protected time for what is valuable; staleness noted.

items.name: Closeness
items.fundamental_question: “I am alive—do I like this fact?”
anchor_quotes: “Can I feel close… Can I admit the closeness of someone else?”
“This ‘to turn to’ will make life vibrate within me… my consent to being touched is necessary.”
indicators_present: Readiness to be moved; admits closeness; “get into touch.”
Life “vibrates within me.”
items.active_stance_quote: “My consent to being touched (to feeling) is necessary.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Open, sustained closeness and affective responsiveness.

items.name: Attention
items.fundamental_question: “I am myself—may I be like this?”
anchor_quotes: “By whom am I seen? Who considers my uniqueness and respects my boundaries?”
“Encounter represents the necessary bridge…”
indicators_present: Feels seen; boundaries respected; genuine encounter.
Emerging “authenticity… relief… self-respect… self-esteem.”
indicators_absent: “Solitude… need to hide behind the shame.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Consistent feeling of being seen with self-affirming stance.

items.name: Justice
items.fundamental_question: “I am myself—may I be like this?”
anchor_quotes: “Do people do me justice?”
Active: “to delimitate myself and to stand by my own… refuse whatever does not correspond to myself.”
indicators_present: Fair treatment; sets boundaries; stands by own.
Can “say ‘yes to myself’.”
items.active_stance_quote: “Stand by my own… refuse whatever does not correspond to myself.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable sense of justice with consistent ethical self-positioning.

items.name: Appreciation
items.fundamental_question: “I am myself—may I be like this?”
anchor_quotes: “For what am I appreciated—for what can I appreciate myself?… The sum of these experiences builds… the self-esteem.”
indicators_present: States recognition/value; “authenticity… relief… self-respect.”
Entitled to be what I am; stands by uniqueness.

scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable appreciation (self and others) with self-respect.

items.name: Field of activity
items.fundamental_question: “I am there—for what is it good?”
anchor_quotes: “Is there a place where I feel needed, where I can be productive?”
If fulfilled: capable of “dedication and action.”
indicators_present: Clear arena of contribution; feels needed/productive.
Acts with dedication and action.
items.active_stance_quote: “Examine whether what I am doing is really a good thing.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable field with sustained contribution and dedication.

items.name: Structural context
items.fundamental_question: “I am there—for what is it good?”
anchor_quotes: “Do I see and experience myself in a larger context that provides structure and orientation… Where I want to be integrated?”
Illustrative stance: “I am working at a cathedral.”
indicators_present: Frames activity within wider purposes/communities; orientation/integration.
Brings self “into agreement with the situation.”
items.active_stance_quote: “Attitude of openness… ‘What does this hour want from me…?’”

scoring_guidance.2_3: Some context awareness without consistent integration.
scoring_guidance.4_5: Clear, lived orientation within a larger whole.

items.name: Value to be realized (future)
items.fundamental_question: “I am there—for what is it good?”
anchor_quotes: “Is there anything that should still be realized in my life?”
Meaning = “the most valuable, realistic possibility… for which I feel I should decide myself.”
indicators_present: Names a specific to-be-realized value/possibility.
Lives a phenomenological “attitude of openness” to what is asked now.
items.active_stance_quote: “What does this hour want from me, how shall I respond?… examine whether what I am doing is really a good thing.”

scoring_guidance.2_3: Vague or unstable aims; goodness not examined.
scoring_guidance.4_5: Specific, realistic value embraced with reflective openness.

negative_indicators_and_coping.E1_World: If unmet → “restlessness, insecurity and fear”; coping: “avoidance or flight… overactivity… destructive aggression… denial.”
negative_indicators_and_coping.E2_Life: If unmet → “longing… coldness… depression”; basic coping: “regression.”
negative_indicators_and_coping.E3_Person: If unmet → “solitude… shame… (histrionic symptoms/personality disorders).” Typical aggression: “indignation, annoyance – anger, reproach.”
negative_indicators_and_coping.E4_Meaning: If unmet → “emptiness, frustration, even despair… frequently addiction.” Freezing forms: “fatalism… apathy… nihilism.”

apa_citations: Längle, A. (2002). Existential fundamental motivation. In Motivation, Consciousness and Self-Regulation (pp. 27–42). Nova Science Publishers.

Längle, A. (2011). The Existential Fundamental Motivations Structuring the Motivational Process. In D. A. Leontiev (Ed.), Motivation, Consciousness and Self-Regulation (pp. 27–42). Nova Science Publishers.
